description:
A research scientist is trying to keep track of how many data records are stored inside each experimental dataset.  
You already have a working copy of the scientist’s data in the following directory tree:

/home/user/data_experiments/  
├── dataset_A/  
│   ├── file1.csv  
│   └── file2.csv  
└── dataset_B/  
    └── measurements.csv  

The scientist would like you to create a single plain-text report named  

/home/user/output/dataset_summary.log  

that summarises how many CSV files and how many data rows are present in every top-level dataset directory.

Required output format (exactly, line-for-line, including capitalisation and the colon delimiter):  

DATASET: <dataset_name>  
FILES: <number_of_csv_files>  
TOTAL_ROWS: <total_rows_across_all_those_files>  
--- end dataset ---  

(repeat the four-line block once for every dataset, in lexicographic order of dataset directory names).  
After the last “--- end dataset ---” block, add one additional line:

SUMMARY_TOTAL_ROWS: <grand_total_rows_in_all_datasets>

Notes and constraints the grader will test:
1. The dataset directories are *only* those that currently exist directly under /home/user/data_experiments (presently dataset_A and dataset_B).  
2. “Rows” means **data rows only**—do *not* count the CSV header lines.  
3. The file /home/user/output/dataset_summary.log must be created (or overwritten if it exists) and must contain exactly the lines described above; no extra blank lines or spaces.  
4. All paths are case-sensitive and must match exactly.  
5. You should accomplish this in a typical Linux shell using no more than a handful of standard commands (approximately three to four commands is sufficient).  
6. Do not hard-code any numbers; the grader will recompute row counts to ensure your script genuinely analysed the files.  

Once the report is generated, display its contents to the terminal so the scientist can immediately see the summary.
truth:
Initial file system state before the agent starts:

1. Directories
   /home/user/data_experiments/
   /home/user/data_experiments/dataset_A/
   /home/user/data_experiments/dataset_B/
   /home/user/output/      (exists and is writable by the user)

2. CSV files and their contents
   a) /home/user/data_experiments/dataset_A/file1.csv  
      id,value  
      1,2.5  
      2,3.1  

      (header + 2 data rows)

   b) /home/user/data_experiments/dataset_A/file2.csv  
      id,value  
      1,7.2  
      2,5.4  
      3,6.0  

      (header + 3 data rows)

   c) /home/user/data_experiments/dataset_B/measurements.csv  
      time,reading  
      00:00,0.12  
      01:00,0.11  
      02:00,0.15  
      03:00,0.10  

      (header + 4 data rows)

Ground-truth values the grader will expect inside the finished log file:

/home/user/output/dataset_summary.log must contain *exactly* (including final newline):

DATASET: dataset_A
FILES: 2
TOTAL_ROWS: 5
--- end dataset ---
DATASET: dataset_B
FILES: 1
TOTAL_ROWS: 4
--- end dataset ---
SUMMARY_TOTAL_ROWS: 9

(There is a final newline character at the end of the last line.)

These values correspond to:
 dataset_A → 2 CSV files, 2+3 = 5 data rows  
 dataset_B → 1 CSV file, 4 data rows  
 Grand total → 9 data rows
